passages.explanation: Paul addresses the crowd in Aramaic — and they grow even quieter. He establishes his credentials: I'm a Jew, born in Tarsus but brought up in this city, educated under Gamaliel in the strictest manner of our law, zealous for God just as all of you are today. He persecuted this Way to the death, arresting and imprisoning both men and women. The high priest and the whole council of elders can testify to this — they gave him letters to the brothers in Damascus to bring believers bound to Jerusalem for punishment. Paul isn't distancing himself from his audience; he's saying: I was exactly like you.

Paul recounts his Damascus road experience: about noon, a bright light from heaven suddenly flashed around him. He fell to the ground and heard a voice: Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? He asked who was speaking. The answer: I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom you are persecuting. His companions saw the light but didn't understand the voice. Paul asked: what shall I do, Lord? Jesus told him to go into Damascus, where he'd be told everything he's been assigned to do. Blinded by the brilliant light, he was led by the hand into the city. Paul adds details not in chapter 9 — the noonday timing emphasises the supernatural brightness, and asking 'what shall I do?' frames his conversion as immediate obedience.

A man named Ananias — devout, respected by all the Jews in Damascus — came to Paul and said: Brother Saul, receive your sight. At that moment Paul could see again. Ananias told him: the God of our ancestors has chosen you to know his will, to see the Righteous One, and to hear words from his mouth. You will be his witness to all people of what you've seen and heard. And now, why are you waiting? Get up, be baptised, and wash your sins away, calling on his name. Paul emphasises Ananias' Jewish piety for this audience — this wasn't some renegade, it was a devout Jew acting on God's direct instructions.

Paul describes a vision he had later while praying in the temple in Jerusalem: the Lord appeared and told him to leave Jerusalem quickly, because they won't accept his testimony about Jesus. Paul protested: Lord, these people know I went from synagogue to synagogue imprisoning and beating those who believe in you. When the blood of your witness Stephen was shed, I stood there giving my approval and guarding the clothes of those who killed him. The Lord's response was final: go — I will send you far away to the Gentiles. Paul is telling the crowd: I didn't want to leave. I argued. But the Lord himself sent me to the Gentiles. It's not Paul's choice — it's God's commission.

The crowd listens until the word 'Gentiles' — then they erupt. They shout: rid the earth of him! He's not fit to live! They throw off their cloaks and fling dust in the air. The commander orders Paul taken inside and flogged to find out why the crowd is so hostile. As they stretch Paul out to flog him, he asks the centurion: is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who hasn't been found guilty? The centurion reports this to the commander, who comes and asks Paul directly. Paul confirms he's a citizen — by birth, not purchase. The commander admits he paid a large sum for his citizenship. Those preparing to flog Paul withdraw immediately, and the commander himself is alarmed because he's already had a Roman citizen bound. Citizenship saves Paul from torture — again. His identity operates on multiple levels: Jew, Christian, and Roman.

The next day the commander wants to find out exactly why Paul is being accused by the Jews. He releases Paul, orders the chief priests and the full Sanhedrin to assemble, and brings Paul before them. The Roman commander is trying to get to the bottom of this — Roman justice requires clarity about the charge before punishment can proceed.
